title: Centralisation and decentralisation
summary: 9609 A Level — central vs decentral decision-making, factors affecting choice, and IT-enabled hybrid models.
learningObjectives: Recommend centralisation level for case firms.

simpleExplanation.title: Who decides?
simpleExplanation.summary: Centralisation keeps major decisions at HQ for consistency and economies of scale. Decentralisation pushes authority to branches or divisions for faster local response.
steps: Central: consistent policy, purchasing economies, tight control.
Decentral: faster local decisions, motivated branch managers.
Growth and geographic spread often trigger decentralisation.
Modern IT allows central monitoring with local autonomy.

flashcards.back: Authority delegated to sub-units or regional managers.
Consistency, bulk buying, unified brand, easier control.
Local knowledge, speed, manager development, motivation.
Crisis, cost-cutting, strong brand standards, small firm.
Diverse markets, rapid local competition, large geography.
ERP dashboards let HQ monitor while branches decide (9.3.3).
International strategy often needs decentralised marketing (7.1.1).

sections.content: **Centralisation and decentralisation** (7.1.5) — common **12–20 mark** theme. Use **case geography, product range, and crisis context** to justify your recommendation.

sections.question: Fast-food chain with 800 franchised outlets wants uniform brand but franchisees demand local menu items. Discuss centralisation vs decentralisation of menu decisions.
sections.solution:
**Central case:** **Brand consistency** — customers expect same core menu; **central purchasing** lowers food cost.

**Decentral case:** **Local tastes** (spicy options, religious dietary needs) boost **local sales** (3.1.5 segmentation).

**Hybrid (typical):** **Core menu central**; **10% local items** approved by regional manager — **controlled decentralisation**.

**Controls:** **Central food safety standards**; franchisees **cannot** change recipes without approval.
